failure_surface: Excel formulas written to target cells are syntactically invalid for Excel execution; specifically, range comparisons like 'B21=$F$5:$F$12' are not supported in standard Excel formulas without array operations. Additionally, verification shows source input cells contain None/empty values.
root_cause: The agent constructed Excel formulas using direct cell-to-range comparisons (e.g., 'IF(cell=range,...)') which is invalid Excel syntax. Excel requires MATCH/COUNTIF/SUMPRODUCT for searching within ranges, not direct equality comparisons. Furthermore, the agent did not verify that source data cells contained actual values before generating formulas dependent on them.
minimal_fix_hypothesis: Before writing formulas to cells, validate that all referenced source cells contain non-empty values. Use only Excel-compatible formula patterns (MATCH for row finding, INDEX for value retrieval, COUNTIF for existence checks) rather than attempting direct cell-to-range comparisons. Test formula validity by loading the saved file and checking for errors.
evidence: Verification output shows: Input values: Rep=None, Client=None for all three target rows, indicating source cells were empty
Formula pattern used: 'IF(B21=$F$5:$F$12,...)' which attempts direct comparison of a cell to a range - invalid Excel syntax
First code attempt failed with Python SyntaxError due to f-string backslash handling, showing insufficient code quality review before retry
Second attempt produced valid Python code but logically invalid Excel formulas that would return #VALUE! errors in Excel

memory_items.title: Validate Source Data Before Formula Generation
memory_items.lesson: Always check that source/reference cells contain actual values before generating formulas that depend on them. Read cell values and log them during formula construction.
memory_items.generalization: When writing dynamic formulas, add validation steps to confirm referenced cells have data. Log cell contents before formula generation to catch missing dependencies early.

memory_items.title: Use Excel-Compatible Lookup Patterns
memory_items.lesson: Avoid direct cell-to-range comparisons in formulas (e.g., 'IF(cell=range)'). Use MATCH, COUNTIF, SUMPRODUCT, or INDEX/MATCH combinations that Excel properly supports for range searches.
memory_items.generalization: For multi-column lookups, build formulas using MATCH for position finding and INDEX for value retrieval. Avoid nested IF chains with range comparisons that Excel cannot evaluate.

memory_items.title: Post-Write Formula Validation
memory_items.lesson: After saving a workbook with new formulas, reopen it and verify formulas execute without errors. Check both formula text and calculated results where possible.
memory_items.generalization: Include a validation step that loads the saved file and checks target cells for formula errors (#VALUE!, #REF!, etc.) before confirming task completion.

edits.content:
## 6. Formula Construction Safety
- **Source Validation**: Before writing formulas, verify all referenced source cells contain non-empty values. Log values during construction.
- **Range Comparison Rules**: Never use direct cell-to-range equality checks (e.g., 'IF(A1,$B$1:$B$10)') in formulas. Use MATCH, COUNTIF, or INDEX/MATCH for range searches.
- **Post-Writing Verification**: Reload the saved workbook and check target cells for formula execution errors before confirming success.
- **Error Logging**: Report any cells with formula errors (#VALUE!, #REF!, etc.) in final status.
